title: Slow-cooker lamb curry
subtitle: Shoulder of lamb in a rich, aromatic sauce, eight hours low.
excerpt: Diced lamb shoulder marinated in yoghurt and spices, then slow-cooked with onions, ginger, garlic and tomato for eight hours until meltingly tender. The sauce thickens as the yoghurt cooks down. Serve with basmati rice and naan. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Anglo-Indian slow-cooked lamb curry. The yoghurt marinade technique draws on North Indian mutton curry traditions as documented in Madhur Jaffrey's Indian Cookery (1982) and Camellia Panjabi's 50 Great Curries of India (1994).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze for up to three months. Defrost overnight; reheat gently on the hob with a splash of water.
recipe.batchNotes: Makes excellent leftovers; the flavour deepens on day two.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the day before. Reheat on the hob or in a low oven at 160°C covered for 30 minutes.

recipeTools.notes: For browning the lamb and cooking the masala base.

content.text: Lamb shoulder is the right cut for this. It has enough fat and connective tissue to withstand eight hours in a slow cooker without drying out; the fat bastes the meat from within, and the connective tissue dissolves into the sauce. The yoghurt marinade acts both as a tenderiser and as a thickener for the sauce.

The optional overnight marinade improves the flavour and is worth it if you plan ahead. At minimum, marinate for two hours. The spice base is simple: the complexity comes from time, not from dozens of whole spices.

items.prepNote: trimmed of excess fat, cut into 4 cm chunks

content.text: Combine {{lamb-shoulder}} of lamb chunks with {{plain-yoghurt}} of plain yoghurt, {{garam-masala}} of garam masala, {{turmeric}} of turmeric and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt in a bowl. Mix well until the lamb is coated. Cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours, or overnight.

Heat {{rapeseed-oil}} of oil in a large frying pan over medium-high heat. Remove the lamb from the marinade (reserve the marinade) and brown in batches for 3 minutes per side. Transfer to the slow cooker. In the same pan, cook {{onion}} chopped onions for 8 minutes until dark golden. Add {{garlic}} of grated garlic, {{ginger-root}} of grated ginger and {{curry-powder}} of curry powder. Cook for 2 minutes. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes and the reserved marinade; stir and pour over the lamb.

Cook on LOW for 7 to 8 hours or HIGH for 4 to 5 hours until the lamb is very tender and falling apart. Taste and adjust seasoning. Scatter with fresh coriander if using. Serve with basmati rice and warm naan.

items.cause: Temperature too low, or the shoulder was bone-in and the timing was for boned.
items.fix: Add 2 hours on LOW. A bone-in shoulder needs an extra hour or two.
Cook on LOW rather than HIGH; the yoghurt is more likely to split at HIGH temperatures. Stir in a tablespoon of double cream to rescue a split sauce.

items.cause: Lamb not trimmed and excess fat rendered into the sauce.
items.fix: Skim the surface fat with a spoon before serving, or refrigerate overnight and lift the solidified fat off the top.

content.text: Anglo-Indian lamb curry is the dish that arrived in British homes through the spice trade, the empire, and the great postwar wave of South Asian migration. This version is a slow-cooker adaptation: practical, affordable, and far better on day two. Serve for a Friday dinner or set it on a Saturday morning for an easy Sunday feast.
